next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

By the time Thea carried the county inventory sheet into the schoolhouse, the room had already begun to look less like a place children used than a room somebody meant to empty. The blackboard still held a pale fan of chalk where she had scrubbed out sums three days ago, but the desks stood in uneven ranks now, two of them already gone home with families who had asked whether the district would mind if they took what had once belonged to their own children. The map of the United States had been rolled up and tied with string. The flag lay on her desk like a folded shirt waiting to be claimed by a body larger than the one it had fit.

The county sheet called the closure a suspension pending consolidation. It made the thing sound temporary, almost courteous. Thea had read the line three times the evening it arrived and still wanted to take a penknife to the word suspension. Nothing in Morrow had been suspended. It had been starved. The school had not closed on one day. It had thinned one desk at a time, one set of overshoes left too long under a bench, one child absent for planting and then absent for good, one family westbound under quilts and rope and dust.

She set her satchel by the stove, unpinned her hat, and opened the east windows as far as the swollen frames allowed. Heat came in anyway. It had a baked, dirty smell, as if the road outside were being toasted and ground at once. Dust lay in the window tracks, in the grooves of the floorboards, in the leather strap around the globe's equator. When she drew her finger along the ledge it made a clean line an...
open_hooks_to_carry: The chapter works as aftermath and inventory rather than another public showdown.
Dell's injury has ongoing bodily specifics: pain, fever, dependence, humiliation.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ack braced against the sideboard. Thea passed Dell's good arm over Cora's shoulders and then came up after him without asking where she was meant to ride. There was barely room. She made room anyway, one hand under the sling to keep the broken arm from swinging, the other pressed flat over the feed sack at Dell's ribs every time the wagon shifted as if she could hold the pain in place by force.

From that close Dell could smell dust in her hair and starch gone damp in her blouse. Cora's thigh was hard against his. Neither woman seemed aware anymore of how their bodies had arranged themselves around him, only of the fact that they had. On the ground below, August stood with his hat in one hand, looking up at them the way he looked at ledgers when a number had gone missing and he could not yet prove theft. Hester saw him looking. The corner of her mouth sharpened.

"You'll be late to the bank," she said.

August put the hat back on. "This is not finished."

"No," Hester said. "It isn't."

The wagon lurched forward. Pain ran bright from Dell's left arm to the base of his neck and settled behind his eyes. He bit it down and watched the basin slide away in pieces between the jolts: the black seam of seep at the trench bottom, the tools leaned careless in the spoil, the little crowd still held there by water and memory and the knowledge that the ground under all of Morrow might remember more than any of them could afford. He had wanted the place opened. He had wanted it enough to mistake urgency for skill. Saint's Basin had opened all right. It had only taken its payment in bone.
state_deltas_required: Keep Dell alive, contain the household after the dig, and decide whether the seep is worth any more sacrifice.
Cora chooses Dell's survival over the farm for the first time, which loosens her hold on Morrow even before any formal leaving plan exists.
